# IELTS Speaking — Assessor Rubric

> **Document type:** COURSE_REFERENCE_ASSESSOR_RUBRIC · **Intended assertion category:** `assessment_guidance` / `assessment_approach` (INSTRUCTION_CATEGORIES) · **LO systemRole if generated:** `ASSESSOR_RUBRIC` · **Question assessmentUse if generated:** `TUTOR_ONLY` · **Audience: assessor / scoring loop only**
>
> This document defines the band descriptors used to **score** the learner. It is not learner-facing content. Nothing here becomes a learning objective the learner is taught directly, and nothing here becomes an MCQ. The tutor uses these descriptors silently in the background to score, and references them in coaching feedback by quoting specific descriptor language ("at Band 7 the examiner expects ...").

The IELTS Speaking test is assessed on **four criteria of equal weight**. Each criterion is scored on a nine-band scale; the four scores are averaged (rounded to the nearest half band) for the overall Speaking band. Score each criterion **independently** before averaging — composite scores hide what needs work.

| # | Criterion | Abbreviation |
|---|-----------|--------------|
| 1 | Fluency and Coherence | FC |
| 2 | Lexical Resource | LR |
| 3 | Grammatical Range and Accuracy | GRA |
| 4 | Pronunciation | P |

A candidate can legitimately score Band 7 on one criterion and Band 5 on another. The rubric is designed to surface those splits.

---

## RUB-FC: Fluency and Coherence — band descriptors

> Definition: the ability to talk with normal levels of continuity, rate and effort, and to link ideas and language together to form coherent, connected speech.

| Band | Descriptor |
|------|-----------|
| 9 | Fluent with only very occasional repetition or self-correction. Hesitation is content-related, not language-search. Speech is situationally appropriate, fully cohesive, fully extended. |
| 8 | Fluent with only very occasional repetition or self-correction. Hesitation is mostly content-related. Topic development is coherent, appropriate, relevant. |
| 7 | Keeps going and produces long turns without noticeable effort. Some hesitation, repetition or self-correction may occur mid-sentence but does not affect coherence. Flexible use of discourse markers and cohesive features. |
| 6 | Keeps going and produces long turns. Coherence may be lost at times due to hesitation, repetition or self-correction. Uses a range of discourse markers though not always appropriately. |
| 5 | Usually keeps going but relies on repetition / self-correction / slow speech. Hesitations associated with mid-sentence searches for basic lexis or grammar. Overuse of certain discourse markers. |
| 4 | Unable to keep going without noticeable pauses. Speech may be slow with frequent repetition. Often self-corrects. Links simple sentences with repetitious connectives. Some breakdowns in coherence. |
| 3 | Frequent, sometimes long pauses. Limited ability to link simple sentences and go beyond simple responses. Frequently unable to convey basic message. |
| 2 | Lengthy pauses before nearly every word. Speech of virtually no communicative significance. |
| 1 | Essentially none. Speech is totally incoherent. |
| 0 | Does not attend / does not complete. |

---

## RUB-LR: Lexical Resource — band descriptors

> Definition: the ability to use vocabulary with flexibility and precision, including less-common and idiomatic items.

| Band | Descriptor |
|------|-----------|
| 9 | Total flexibility and precise use in all contexts. Sustained, accurate use of idiomatic and less-common vocabulary. |
| 8 | Wide vocabulary used readily and flexibly to convey precise meaning. Skilful use of less-common and idiomatic vocabulary despite occasional inaccuracy. Effective paraphrase. |
| 7 | Vocabulary resources flexibly used to discuss a variety of topics. Some less-common and idiomatic vocabulary, with awareness of style and collocation, despite occasional inappropriate choice. Effective paraphrase. |
| 6 | Wide enough vocabulary to discuss topics at length and make meaning clear despite inappropriate choice. Generally successful paraphrase. |
| 5 | Manages to talk about familiar and unfamiliar topics but with limited flexibility. Attempts paraphrase but with mixed success. |
| 4 | Vocabulary adequate to talk about familiar topics but only basic meaning on unfamiliar topics. Frequent inappropriate choice. Rarely attempts paraphrase. |
| 3 | Simple vocabulary used to convey personal information. Insufficient vocabulary for less-familiar topics. |
| 2 | Only produces isolated words or memorised utterances. |
| 1 | No usable communicative vocabulary. |
| 0 | Does not attend / does not complete. |

---

## RUB-GRA: Grammatical Range and Accuracy — band descriptors

> Definition: the ability to use a range of grammatical structures accurately and flexibly in speech.

| Band | Descriptor |
|------|-----------|
| 9 | Full range of structures used flexibly. Produces consistently accurate structures apart from slips characteristic of native-speaker speech. |
| 8 | Wide range of structures used flexibly. Majority of sentences error-free with only very occasional inappropriacies or basic / non-systematic errors. |
| 7 | Range of complex structures with some flexibility. Frequently produces error-free sentences though some grammatical mistakes persist. |
| 6 | Mix of short and complex structures, but with limited flexibility. Errors with complex structures rarely cause comprehension problems. |
| 5 | Basic sentence forms produced with reasonable accuracy. Limited range of more complex structures, which usually contain errors and may cause comprehension problems. |
| 4 | Basic sentence forms and some correct simple sentences but subordinate structures are rare. Errors are frequent and may lead to misunderstanding. |
| 3 | Basic sentence forms attempted with limited success or relies on apparently memorised utterances. Numerous errors except in memorised expressions. |
| 2 | Cannot produce basic sentence forms. |
| 1 | None usable. |
| 0 | Does not attend / does not complete. |

---

## RUB-P: Pronunciation — band descriptors

> Definition: the ability to be understood with ease, using a range of pronunciation features (stress, rhythm, intonation, connected speech). Note: a recognisable L1 accent is **not** itself a deduction; intelligibility is.

| Band | Descriptor |
|------|-----------|
| 9 | Uses a full range of pronunciation features with precision and subtlety. Sustains flexible use of features throughout. Effortless to understand. |
| 8 | Uses a wide range of pronunciation features. Sustains flexible use of features, with only occasional lapses. Easy to understand throughout; L1 accent has minimal effect on intelligibility. |
| 7 | Shows all the positive features of Band 6, and some but not all the positive features of Band 8. |
| 6 | Uses a range of pronunciation features with mixed control. Shows some effective use of features but this is not sustained. Generally understood throughout, though mispronunciation of individual words or sounds reduces clarity at times. |
| 5 | Shows all positive features of Band 4 and some but not all positive features of Band 6. |
| 4 | Uses a limited range of pronunciation features. Attempts to control features but lapses are frequent. Mispronunciations are frequent and cause some difficulty for the listener. |
| 3 | Shows some of the features of Band 2 and some but not all the positive features of Band 4. |
| 2 | Speech is often unintelligible. |
| 1 | Cannot produce comprehensible speech. |
| 0 | Does not attend / does not complete. |

---

## Scoring rules (assessor-only)

| Rule | Why |
|------|-----|
| Score each criterion separately and surface the four-number breakdown internally before averaging | Composite scores hide the criterion that's holding the learner back |
| Always cite a specific descriptor phrase when justifying a score | A number without rubric language teaches nothing |
| Do not surface the four-number breakdown to the learner on Call 1 | See course-ref.md → First Call Special Rules |
| Round to the nearest half band only when reporting the overall | Half bands carry meaning; quarter bands do not |
| Re-score after every coaching cycle that ends in a re-attempt | The point of the cycle is to demonstrate movement on one criterion |

---

## Compression rules for tutor delivery

When the tutor verbalises a score to the learner (Call 2 onwards), use this format and **only** this format:

> "Your [criterion] is around Band [X] because [evidence from the learner's response]. At Band [X+1], the examiner expects [descriptor language for the next band]. Let's try [the targeted re-attempt]."

Anything more elaborate becomes a lecture; anything less specific becomes "good answer", which violates the course's first principle.
